title: 10 best herbal tinctures for bad breath

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties, which help to eliminate bacteria that cause bad breath. The menthol in peppermint also helps to freshen the mouth and throat by stimulating saliva production, which washes away bacteria and food particles that can contribute to bad breath. Additionally, peppermint's cooling effect on the tongue and roof of the mouth can also help to reduce the sensation of dryness, further reducing the likelihood of bad breath.
remedy_properties: Digestive aid: Peppermint oil's carminative and anti-inflammatory properties help soothe digestive issues, reducing the likelihood of halitosis caused by poor digestion or gum inflammation.

Antibacterial properties: Peppermint's menthol content has antibacterial effects that can help combat oral bacteria responsible for bad breath, reducing the formation of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath.
Mouthwash-like effects: The tincture's antimicrobial and astringent properties can help kill bacteria on contact, leaving a fresh, clean sensation in the mouth that helps eliminate bad breath.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Menthol helps with bad breath by its decongestant and antimicrobial properties, which reduce inflammation and kill bacteria that cause bad breath.
Menthone: Menthone, a monoterpene ketone, has antimicrobial and antifungal properties that help combat the growth of bacteria and fungi in the mouth, contributing to bad breath.

Rosmarinic acid: Rosmarinic acid, a phenolic compound, has antioxidant and antimicrobial properties that help reduce oxidative stress and prevent the growth of bacteria and fungi that cause bad breath.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are the most commonly used part of Mentha x piperita due to their high concentration of menthol and menthone, which have antibacterial properties that help combat bad breath.
Roots: The roots are used for their ability to promote saliva production, which helps to neutralize acids in the mouth and reduce bad breath.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes are used for their antiseptic properties, which help to reduce bacterial growth in the mouth and alleviate bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh mentha x piperita leaves and flowers in the morning after dew has evaporated.
Chop the harvested plant material into small pieces using a sharp knife to increase surface area.
Combine the chopped plant material with 80 to 1 ratio of 80 proof vodka in a clean glass jar.
Seal the jar and store it in a cool dark place for 2 to 6 weeks shaking it occasionally.
Strain the liquid mixture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into a clean glass container discarding solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tea tree tinctures helps with bad breath because of its antimicrobial properties, which target and eliminate the bacteria that cause halitosis. The tannins in tea tree essential oil also help to reduce inflammation in the mouth and gums, promoting a healthy oral environment. Additionally, tea tree tincture's antiviral and antibacterial properties help to combat viral infections, such as thrush, which can also contribute to bad breath. By addressing the underlying causes of bad breath, tea tree tinctures provide a natural and effective solution for freshening your breath.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial activity: Tea tree oil has been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria, viruses, and fungi that contribute to bad breath (halitosis), helping to reduce their presence in the mouth.
Anti-inflammatory effects: The terpenes present in tea tree oil have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and inflammation in the gums and tongue, common causes of bad breath.

Antioxidant properties: Tea tree oil's antioxidant properties can help neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and contribute to bad breath by creating a favorable environment for bacterial growth.
remedy_constituents: Cineol (also known as Eucalyptol): Cineol helps to reduce bad breath by its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which help to reduce the growth of bacteria in the mouth.
Cymene: Cymene exhibits antimicrobial properties that help to combat the bacteria that cause bad breath, making it effective in reducing halitosis.

Limonene: Limonene, a terpene found in Melaleuca alternifolia, helps to reduce bad breath by its antimicrobial and antifungal properties, which help to maintain oral health and prevent the growth of bacteria and fungi in the mouth.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are commonly used due to their high oil content, particularly the compound cineole, which has antimicrobial properties to help combat bad breath.
Stems: Stems are used for their high oil content as well, contributing to the production of tinctures that help to reduce bacterial growth and eliminate bad breath.
Barks: Barks are also used for their oil content, which is believed to help reduce the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms that contribute to bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 part of fresh leaves of melaleuca alternifolia on a dry sunny morning.
Chop the harvested leaves into small pieces using a sharp knife.
Combine the chopped leaves with 2 parts of vodka in a clean glass jar.
Steep the mixture in a cool dark place for 2-3 weeks shaking the jar daily.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tasmanian blue gum tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties, which effectively combat the underlying causes of halitosis. The tincture's active compounds, such as eucalyptol and cineole, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that contribute to bad breath, while also freshening the mouth and reducing gum inflammation. As a result, regular use of Tasmanian blue gum tinctures can help eliminate unpleasant odors and leave you with a fresher, cleaner feeling in your mouth.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Tasmanian Blue Gum's antimicrobial compounds, such as eucalyptol and α-pinene, help combat bacteria in the mouth that can cause bad breath by reducing their growth and eliminating them.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The tincture's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation in the gums and mouth tissues, which is a common underlying cause of bad breath.

Astringent and antiseptic properties: Tasmanian Blue Gum's astringent and antiseptic compounds can help dry up excess mucus and saliva, reducing the amount of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that can contribute to bad breath.
remedy_constituents: Cineole (Eucalyptol): This terpene helps with bad breath by reducing the growth of oral bacteria and having antimicrobial properties, thereby minimizing the production of volatile sulfur compounds that cause bad breath.

Catechin: As a type of phenolic compound, catechin helps with bad breath by reducing the production of volatile sulfur compounds and having antimicrobial properties, thereby minimizing the growth of oral bacteria that cause bad breath.

Flavonoids (including Quercetin and Rutin): These compounds help with bad breath by reducing inflammation and having antimicrobial properties, which in turn minimize the growth of oral bacteria and the production of volatile sulfur compounds that cause bad breath.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are the most commonly used part due to their high concentration of eucalyptol, a natural compound with decongestant and antibacterial properties that help combat bad breath.

Barks: The barks of Eucalyptus globulus contain eucalyptol and other compounds that have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in reducing bad breath and gum inflammation.
Buds: Eucalyptus buds contain a high amount of eucalyptol and other essential oils that help to eliminate bacteria and freshen breath, making them a popular choice for tinctures and mouthwashes.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 part fresh or dried leaves of eucalyptus globulus by cutting the stems near the base of the plant.
Chop the harvested leaves into smaller pieces to release their oils and increase their surface area for extraction.
Combine the chopped leaves with 2 parts 95% grain alcohol in a clean glass jar to create the tincture base.
Store the jar in a cool dark place and steep for 2 to 6 weeks shaking the jar daily to ensure even extraction.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into a clean glass bottle to separate the tincture from the plant material.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ceylon cinnamon tinctures helps with bad breath because of its potent antimicrobial properties, which target the underlying causes of halitosis. The tincture's active compounds, including cinnamaldehyde and cinnamic acid, inhibit the growth of bacteria that contribute to bad breath, reducing volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) production in the mouth. Additionally, Ceylon cinnamon's anti-inflammatory properties soothe gum inflammation and reduce plaque formation, further combating bad breath. By addressing the root causes of halitosis, herbal Ceylon cinnamon tinctures offer a natural solution for fresher breath.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial activity: Ceylon cinnamon tincture's antimicrobial properties help to kill bacteria in the mouth and throat that can cause bad breath, reducing their population and eliminating their ability to produce volatile sulfur compounds that contribute to halitosis.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory effects of Ceylon cinnamon tincture may help to reduce swelling and irritation in the gums and tongue, which can contribute to bad breath by creating a favorable environment for bacterial growth.
Antioxidant activity: Antioxidants in Ceylon cinnamon tincture help to neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and contribute to bad breath, promoting overall oral health and freshness.
remedy_constituents: Cinnamaldehyde: A phenolic compound that has antimicrobial properties, helping to reduce the growth of bacteria in the mouth that cause bad breath.
Linalool: A terpene with anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help soothe mouth irritation and reduce the presence of bacteria that contribute to bad breath.
Eugenol: A phenolic compound with antibacterial properties that can help combat the growth of bacteria in the mouth, thereby reducing the causes of bad breath.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves contain compounds that have antimicrobial properties, which help kill bacteria that cause bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 pounds of cinnamomum verum bark in the morning after the dew has evaporated.
Chop the bark into small pieces and dry it in a warm oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Combine the dried bark with 80 proof vodka or glycerin in a 1:5 ratio by weight.
Steep the mixture in a glass container in a cool dark place for 2 weeks.
Strain and filter the liquid through a cheesecloth and store it in a dark glass bottle with a dropper cap.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginger tinctures helps with bad breath because it has natural antibacterial properties that help to kill bacteria in the mouth, which is a common cause of bad breath. The essential oils present in ginger also have anti-inflammatory properties, reducing swelling and redness in the gums and tongue, which can contribute to bad breath. Additionally, ginger tinctures stimulate saliva production, washing away bacteria and debris from the teeth and tongue, leaving the mouth feeling fresh and clean.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Ginger's antimicrobial compounds, such as gingerols and shogaols, help to combat the growth of bacteria in the mouth, which can contribute to bad breath.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Ginger's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as gingerols and shogaols, reduce inflammation in the gums and mucous membranes, which can help alleviate symptoms of gum disease and halitosis (bad breath).
Digestive aid: Ginger's ability to stimulate digestion and relieve nausea can help to prevent the buildup of toxins and debris in the mouth, which can cause bad breath.
remedy_constituents: Gingerols: These sesquiterpene compounds have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and inflammation in the mouth and gums, thereby reducing bad breath.
Shogaols: These ginger-derived compounds have antibacterial properties that can help combat the growth of bacteria in the mouth, which can contribute to bad breath.
Zingiberene: This sesquiterpene has been shown to have antimicrobial properties that can help reduce the levels of bacteria in the mouth, thereby reducing bad breath.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: These are the most commonly used part due to their high gingerol and shogaol content, which have antimicrobial properties to combat bad breath.
Roots: The roots of Zingiber officinale are also used to make tinctures for bad breath, as they contain compounds that help reduce inflammation and fight bacterial growth in the mouth.

Buds: Ginger buds, which are the young, unopened shoots of the plant, are sometimes used in tinctures for bad breath due to their high concentration of bioactive compounds that have antibacterial properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest zingiber officinale roots in the fall after the plant has stopped growing for the season.
Chop 200 grams of the harvested roots into small pieces to increase surface area.
Combine the chopped roots with 400 milliliters of vodka in a clean glass jar.
Steep the mixture in a cool dark place for 2 to 6 weeks, shaking the jar daily.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Coriander tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The tincture contains compounds like cineole and linolool that have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria in the mouth, reducing volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath. Anti-inflammatory properties: Coriander's anti-inflammatory compounds, including flavonoids and phenolic acids, reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can contribute to bad breath and gum disease.

remedy_parts: Seeds: They contain coriandrin, which has antibacterial properties that help combat bad breath-causing bacteria.

remedy_recipe: Harvest coriandrum sativum leaves and stems when they are fresh and in optimal condition for maximum potency retention.
Clean the coriandrum sativum plant material thoroughly using distilled water to remove dirt and debris completely.
Combine 1 part of the cleaned coriandrum sativum plant material with 2 parts of 80-proof vodka in a clean glass jar.
Store the mixture in a cool dark place for 2 to 6 weeks to allow the plant material to infuse into the vodka completely.
Strain the mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle and discard the solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 grams of fresh thymus vulgaris leaves and flowers at peak potency.

Combine the chopped thymus vulgaris with 80-100 proof vodka in a 1:5 ratio by weight.
Steep the mixture in a glass jar for 2-3 weeks in a cool dark place.

Combine the dried satureja hortensis material with 2 parts of high-proof vodka in a clean glass jar.
Store the jar in a cool dark place for 4 weeks shaking it every 2 days to facilitate extraction.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container discarding the solids.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Rosemary's antioxidants help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the mouth, which can contribute to bad breath by breaking down food particles and bacteria.

Antibacterial properties: Rosemary's essential oils have antibacterial effects on various oral bacteria, including those that cause bad breath (halitosis), thereby reducing their numbers and preventing the formation of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh rosmarinus officinalis leaves when they are in their optimal potency usually after morning dew has evaporated.
Chop the rosmarinus officinalis leaves into small pieces to increase their surface area for infusion.
Combine 250 grams of chopped rosmarinus officinalis leaves with 750 milliliters of 80 proof vodka in a clean glass jar.
Seal the jar and store it in a cool dark place for 4 to 6 weeks to allow extraction.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container and discard the solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English lavender tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The active compounds in lavender, such as linalool and linalyl acetate, have been shown to inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria that can cause bad breath. Additionally, lavender's soothing properties help reduce inflammation in the mouth and gums, which can also contribute to unpleasant odors. By using an herbal English lavender tincture, individuals can experience a fresher, cleaner breath without the use of artificial chemicals or additives.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: English Lavender tincture has been shown to possess antimicrobial properties, which can help eliminate bacteria that cause bad breath (halitosis) in the mouth and throat.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in English Lavender tincture can reduce swelling and irritation in the gums, tongue, and mucous membranes, which can contribute to bad breath.

remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, which help to combat bacteria and reduce inflammation in the mouth, making it effective against bad breath.
Flowers: The flowers contain camphor, a natural antiseptic that helps to kill bacteria and freshen breath, making them an ideal choice for creating tinctures.
remedy_recipe: Harvest dried lavender flowers according to your local regulations and store them in an airtight container for later use.
Measure out one part dried lavender flowers by weight to two parts high-proof vodka or glycerin as the menstruum.
Combine the measured lavender flowers with the menstruum in a clean glass jar, ensuring all ingredients are fully submerged, and seal the jar tightly.
Store the jar in a cool dark place for 6-8 weeks, shaking it daily to facilitate extraction of active compounds from the lavender flowers.
After the preparation period, strain the liquid through a coffee filter or cheesecloth into another glass container, discarding the solids, and label the tincture with the date and ingredients used.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sage tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties that target the bacteria responsible for halitosis. The essential oils present in sage, such as thujone and camphor, have antimicrobial effects that help to kill off the bacteria that cause bad breath. Additionally, sage tincture can also help to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can contribute to bad breath. By using herbal sage tinctures regularly, individuals may experience a reduction in their symptoms of bad breath.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Sage's antimicrobial compounds, such as thymol and carnosic acid, help kill bacteria in the mouth that can cause bad breath by reducing the growth of these microorganisms.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Sage's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as rosmarinic acid, help reduce inflammation in the gums and mucous membranes, which can contribute to bad breath by improving oral health and reducing redness and swelling.

Antioxidant properties: Sage's antioxidant compounds, such as flavonoids, help neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and contribute to bad breath by promoting healthy cells and tissue.
remedy_parts: Roots: Sage roots are used to create a tincture that helps to eliminate bacteria and fungi in the mouth, reducing bad breath.
Barks: The barks of the sage plant contain compounds that help to reduce inflammation and kill bacteria, making them effective against bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh salvia officinalis leaves and chop them into small pieces weighing approximately 100-150 grams.
Combine the chopped herb with high-proof vodka or grain alcohol in a clean glass jar at a ratio of 1 part herb to 5 parts liquid.
Seal the jar and store it in a cool dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking the mixture daily to aid infusion.
Strain the tincture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into another clean glass bottle, discarding the solids and retaining approximately 20ml of the liquid.
Label and store the finished tincture in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 years, dosing as needed with 10-15 drops in water or mouthwash twice daily.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The tannins in licorice root have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that can cause bad breath, such as Streptococcus mutans and E. coli. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory properties of licorice help to soothe and heal gums and mucous membranes, reducing inflammation and redness that can contribute to bad breath. By tackling both bacterial growth and inflammation, herbal licorice tinctures provide a natural solution for freshening breath and promoting oral health.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Licorice root has anti-inflammatory compounds like glycyrrhizin and glabridin, which help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, thereby alleviating symptoms of bad breath.

Antimicrobial property: Herbal licorice tinctures contain triterpenoids that exhibit antimicrobial activity against bacteria and viruses, helping to combat the underlying causes of bad breath such as gum disease and oral infections.

Soften and protect mucous membranes property: Licorice root's anti-inflammatory and soothing properties help soften and protect the mucous membranes in the mouth and throat, reducing irritation and inflammation that can contribute to bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the mouth and alleviate bad breath.
Barks: The barks of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, a compound that has antibacterial properties, making it effective against bacteria that cause bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots in the morning after dew has evaporated and sun exposure.
Dry the harvested roots in a warm place away from direct sunlight for at least 7 days or until brittle.
Grind 100 grams of dried glycyrrhiza glabra root into fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Combine the powdered root with 500 milliliters of high-proof vodka and let it steep for 14 days in a glass jar.
Strain the mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another glass container and discard solids after filtering is complete.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Barberry tinctures helps with bad breath because of its antimicrobial properties, which inhibit the growth of bacteria that cause halitosis. Additionally, barberry's astringent properties help to dry out excess saliva and mucous, reducing bacterial proliferation and leaving the mouth feeling fresh and clean. As a result, regular use of herbal barberry tinctures can effectively alleviate persistent bad breath.
remedy_properties: Berberine: A bioactive compound found in barberry, berberine has antimicrobial and antibacterial properties that help combat the bacteria responsible for bad breath (halitosis).

Coptisine: A flavonoid present in barberry, copytisine exhibits anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities that can reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the mouth, contributing to bad breath.

Amygdalin: Another bioactive compound found in barberry, amygdalin has been shown to exhibit antibacterial properties against Streptococcus mutans, a common oral bacteria linked to tooth decay and bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are good for making medicinal tinctures for bad breath because they contain a high concentration of berberine, an alkaloid that has antibacterial properties.
Barks: The barks are used in tincture-making for their ability to reduce inflammation and combat bad breath-causing bacteria.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried berberis vulgaris roots and stems.
Chop the plant material into small pieces weighing approximately 20%.
Combine the chopped plant material with high-proof vodka in a glass jar.
Store the jar in a dark place for four to six weeks allowing maceration.
Strain and filter the liquid through cheesecloth or a coffee filter before bottling.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Turmeric tinctures helps with bad breath because of its potent antimicrobial properties. The active compound curcumin in turmeric has been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that cause halitosis, also known as bad breath. By reducing the amount of these bacteria in the mouth and throat, turmeric tincture can help eliminate foul odors and leave your breath fresh and clean. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory properties may reduce gum inflammation and plaque, further contributing to improved oral health and a more pleasant breath.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial property: Turmeric's antimicrobial compounds, such as curcuminoids, help to reduce the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms in the mouth, which can cause bad breath.
Anti-inflammatory property: Turmeric's anti-inflammatory compounds, including curcumin, help to reduce swelling and irritation in the gums and tongue, which can contribute to bad breath.
Antioxidant property: Turmeric's antioxidant properties help to neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and lead to bad breath, while also promoting overall oral health.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rich in curcuminoids, including curcumin, which has potent antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties that help combat bad breath.
Roots: Contain volatile oils with antimicrobial properties that help reduce the growth of bacteria responsible for bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest and wash fresh turmeric roots for 10-15 minutes under running water.
Dry the cleaned turmeric roots in a warm place away from direct sunlight for 3-4 hours.
Chop the dried turmeric roots into small pieces using scissors or a sharp knife to release oils and resins.
Combine chopped turmeric root with high-proof vodka or everclear at a ratio of 1:2 for 4-6 weeks in a dark glass jar.
Strain the tincture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle, discard solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tinctures helps with bad breath because of its soothing properties that calm inflammation in the mouth and throat. The herb's mucilage, a thick, gel-like substance, coats the tongue, gums, and palate, reducing irritation and discomfort associated with bad breath. Additionally, marshmallow tincture's antimicrobial properties help to eliminate bacteria and other microorganisms that contribute to unpleasant odors. By reducing inflammation and killing bacteria, herbal marshmallow tinctures promote a healthy oral environment, leaving your mouth feeling fresh and clean.
remedy_properties: Mucilages: Marshmallow root contains mucilages, which are thick, protective substances that coat and soothe the mouth and throat tissues, reducing inflammation and irritation that can contribute to bad breath.

Antioxidants: Marshmallow tincture contains antioxidants like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help neutralize free radicals in the mouth and throat that can cause damage and contribute to unpleasant odors.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: Marshmallow root contains anti-inflammatory compounds like saponins and phenolic acids, which can reduce swelling and discomfort in the mouth and throat, helping to eliminate underlying causes of bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, a thick, protective substance that helps soothe and freshen the mouth, reducing bad breath.
Seeds: Marshmallow seeds have been traditionally used to combat halitosis due to their ability to stimulate saliva production, which helps wash away bacteria and debris that cause bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Gather one cup of dried marshmallow root althaea officinalis and clean glass jar.
Combine the dried marshmallow root with high-proof vodka or grain alcohol at a ratio of one part herb to three parts solvent.
Fill the glass jar to the top with the mixture and store it in a cool dark place for four weeks shaking daily.
Strain the tincture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into another clean glass bottle discarding the solids.
Label and store the final tincture in the refrigerator at 45 degrees fahrenheit using within six months.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. Seeds: High in antimicrobial properties, which help to combat oral infections and eliminate odor-causing microorganisms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in the morning after dew has dried off.
Cleanly chop 50 grams of fresh or dried herb material into small pieces using scissors or a food processor.
Combine chopped herbs with 500 milliliters of vodka or rum spirit in a clean glass jar, ensuring complete immersion.
Store the mixture in a cool dark place for 14 to 21 days, shaking daily to facilitate extraction.
Strain the tincture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container, discarding solids and storing the liquid.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemongrass tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties, which combat the underlying causes of halitosis. The citrusy essential oils in lemongrass have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that contribute to bad breath, such as Streptococcus mutans and Porphyromonas gingivalis. Additionally, lemongrass has anti-inflammatory compounds that soothe gums and reduce redness, further alleviating symptoms of bad breath. remedy_properties: Antimicrobial Properties: Lemongrass tincture's antimicrobial properties help to combat the bacteria and germs that cause bad breath, reducing the production of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that contribute to halitosis.

Anti-inflammatory Properties: Lemongrass tincture's anti-inflammatory properties soothe and reduce swelling in the gums and mouth tissues, which can become inflamed and irritated due to poor oral hygiene or underlying health conditions, contributing to bad breath.

Natural Deodorizer and Antioxidant Properties: Lemongrass tincture's natural deodorizing and antioxidant properties help to neutralize and eliminate the volatile compounds responsible for bad breath, while also protecting the mouth tissues from oxidative stress and damage.

Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes contain a high concentration of essential oils that help to freshen and deodorize the mouth, providing relief from bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh cymbopogon citratus leaves in the early morning for maximum potency.
Clean and dry the harvested leaves by gently shaking off excess moisture.
Chop the clean leaves into small pieces and combine with 100 proof vodka in a glass jar filling it to the top.
Store the mixture in a cool dark place for four weeks allowing the tincture to mature slowly.
Strain the mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle discarding the solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold tinctures helps with bad breath because of its antimicrobial properties, which target the bacteria that cause unpleasant odors in the mouth. Seeds: With their high content of fatty acids and vitamins, pot marigold seeds are prized for their ability to soothe gum inflammation and reduce plaque, making them an effective ingredient in tinctures for bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh calendula officinalis flowers in the morning when they are fully open and free of dew.
Chop the harvested flowers into small pieces to release their essential oils and then combine them with 70% ethanol in a clean glass jar.
Steep the mixture for 14 days, shaking the jar daily to ensure all parts of the plant are infused with the solvent.
Strain the tincture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into another clean glass container, discarding the solids and reserving the liquid extract.
Store the calendula officinalis tincture in a cool dark place for at least 6 weeks before use to allow the flavors and aromas to mature.

intro_desc: Herbal tinctures for bad breath are concentrated plant extracts that help alleviate halitosis by combating the underlying causes of bad breath, such as gum disease, dry mouth, and poor oral hygiene. The best herbal tinctures for bad breath include Eucalyptus globulus, Mentha x piperita, Zingiber officinale, Cinnamomum verum, Salvia officinalis, Echinacea purpurea, Melaleuca alternifolia, Syzygium aromaticum, Thymus vulgaris, and Glycyrrhiza glabra. If left untreated, bad breath can lead to social anxiety, low self-esteem, and difficulty in forming close relationships, ultimately affecting one's overall quality of life and well-being.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to bad breath that are treated with herbal tinctures are halitosis-like conditions such as gum inflammation, tooth decay, and mouth sores. Herbal remedies like peppermint, clove, and eucalyptus help reduce oral bacteria, fight infections, and soothe gums. Other conditions treated with herbal tinctures include tonsil stones, gum recession, and dry mouth, all of which can contribute to unpleasant breath odor.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal tinctures that help with bad breath is a blend of peppermint, sage, and myrrh. Peppermint helps to freshen and cool the breath, while sage aids in reducing bacteria and inflammation in the mouth. Myrrh, with its antimicrobial properties, helps to combat the root causes of bad breath. This combination can be taken orally, either sublingually or mixed with water, to help alleviate bad breath and promote a healthy oral environment. Regular use can lead to noticeable improvements in breath quality.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Oral diseases, the application of herbal agents has been suggested as an alternate remedy for oral-dental problems, including bad breath. The study conducted an extensive literature review via the Internet database, PubMed, to examine the current developments in the use of medicinal herbs and phytochemicals in oral and dental health. The results of the study showed that medicinal herbs have been utilized as antimicrobial, antineoplastic, antiseptic, antioxidant, and analgesic agents, indicating their potential efficacy in treating bad breath, which is often caused by microbial imbalances in the mouth.
